items.q: Can a device ever be 100 percent efficient?
items.correct: In practice, no — some energy is always transferred to less useful stores (e.g. heat to surroundings).
items.misconception: A well-designed device can be 100 percent efficient by eliminating all friction.
items.random_wrong: Efficiency above 100 percent is possible if the device extracts ambient energy.
items.why: Students think 'inefficiency' is a design flaw to be removed, missing that the second law of thermodynamics ensures some dissipation.

items.q: What is the difference between energy and power?
items.correct: Energy is measured in joules; power is the rate of energy transfer, measured in watts (joules per second).
items.misconception: Energy and power are the same — both describe how much fuel something uses.
items.random_wrong: Power is the energy used over the lifetime of a device.
items.why: Students use 'power' in everyday language to mean 'a lot of energy', missing that power has the dimension of energy per time.

items.q: Why does increasing the intensity of light below the threshold frequency not eject electrons?
items.correct: Each photon's energy is below the work function; many low-energy photons can't combine to eject an electron.
items.misconception: Enough low-frequency photons should eventually eject an electron if you wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Low-frequency light doesn't have enough mass to push electrons out of the metal.
items.why: Students think energy is cumulative (more photons = more energy delivered), missing that each electron interacts with one photon at a time in the photoelectric effect.
Correct answer: change in momentum. “mass” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's x4. “x2” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
